The Tracer Transport Module Part II Daniel Reinert and Günther Zängl
The Tracer Transport Module
Part II: Description and Validation of the Vertical Transport
Operator
Daniel Reinert and Günther Zängl
Deutscher Wetterdienst
This document refers to release icon-2.6.2.
Abstract
We describe the mathematical operator for vertical scalar transport in ICON's tracer
transport module. The specic form of the operator follows the so called Flux Form Semi-
Lagrangian (FFSL) approach. The time integral of the instantaneous tracer mass ux that
emerges in any standard nite volume form of the tracer mass continuity equation, is recast
into an integral in space along 1 D backward trajectories (the characteristics). Albeit being
fully explicit in time, this approach allows stable time integration for Courant numbers greater
than one. The unknown tracer subgrid distribution is approximated by piecewise parabolic
interpolants, which makes the scheme equivalent to the well known third order PPM method.
A variant of PPM, known as the PSM method, has also been implemented and compared
to PPM in a series of idealized transport tests. It is shown that PSM gives slightly but
consistently better results than PPM in terms of l 2 and l 1 error norms.
Monotonicity preserving reconstruction limiters are presented for PPM and PSM, which
avoid the occurrence of spurious extrema in the vicinity of discontinuities or marginally re-
solved gradients. In order to reduce the tendency of those limiters to damp smooth (physical)
extrema, a more selective detection algorithm from the literature has been tested. The rened
detection algorithm is shown to better discriminate between cells where limiting is necessary
and where it should be avoided and, hence, eciently reduces the damping of smooth extrema.
Performance results are presented for the NEC SX AURORA, which provide insight into
the relative cost of the available limiter options and transport operators. It is shown that
semi-monotonic PSM is approximately 19 % more expensive than semi-monotonic PPM and
that the semi-monotonic limiter together with the selective detection algorithm can be a
cost-ecient choice for time-critical real case applications.
Keywords: vertical transport, nite volume, FFSL, PPM, PSM, reconstruction limiting
Correspondence to: Daniel Reinert (daniel.reinert@dwd.de)

catalog_entry.summary: This paper describes and validates the vertical transport operator used in ICON's tracer transport module, focusing on the Flux Form Semi-Lagrangian approach and comparing Piecewise Parabolic Method (PPM) with Parabolic Spline Method (PSM).
